speaker: interviewer
text: My name-- [AUDIO OUT] I am conducting an interview with Israel Kipen in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia, on the 17th of February, 1997. This is the first tape with Israel Kipen. Can you please tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Israel Kipen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-I-P-E-N. Israel, I-S-R-A-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on the 21st of March, 1919, in Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me where you were born, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-I-A-L-Y-S-T-O-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Where did you live in Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in Bialystok at the very heart of the city. The address, I recollect three addresses for my years. The first two or three years of my life was at they-- they call now Rozanska and Kupiecka Street on the second floor in an apartment. Then we shifted to Number 7 Kupiecka Street. It was a much larger home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose five. And some 12, 13 years later, we shifted to Number 19 Kupiecka, in the same street, a little bit further.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who lived with you in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our home lived Father and Mother, my brothers and sister, and my grandmother, my paternal grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any house help that lived with you, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. At a-- at the second home, not in the first. In the second home, there was a domestic living with us, a white Russian woman by the name Manya. And she was part of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Israel, can you describe your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- I'm referring now to Kupiecka 7 now. Kupiecka 7 was a reasonably big apartment if it would be considered in terms of just living quarters. But Kupiecka 7 was more than the living quarters for the-- for our family. It was also the business and factory, all included in the same premises.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- we had a huge room there, a real salon. But that was given over to the--to the machinery. Knitting machines. The other rooms were-- were shared between living and business. That included the dining room, and the dining room table was also the-- the display counter on which the merchandise were displayed because we were in the unique situation that we were manufacturing and retailing. We did not sell wholesale. So the-- the people, especially peasants from the entire locality around knew that address and came-- came to us directly to buy their sweaters whenever they could afford one, and that wasn't too often. What were the sleeping arrangements?

speaker: interviewee
text: The sleeping arrangements were-- well, Father and Mother had the bedroom to themselves. But part of the other space in their room was taken up with-- with bundles of yarn because there was no room to-- in the-- in the olden days, you didn't get yarn on spools. You got them only in hanks even-- even if you used it for industrial purposes. We ourselves had to rewind from the hanks onto spools, and we had in that big room, the first one that I have described, had 12 spindle winding machine. Big, very big thing considering it was a private home, which did just that. Spindles and we pushed on the hanks on it, and then put on a-- a-- a [? electrical ?] in moving spools to take the yarn down from the hanks because you couldn't put them on to machine from-- from the hanks. It had to go on the spools to have an even flow of yarn for the manufacture. Excuse me. Then the other rooms were also to such another degree shared between the-- the quarters of-- there was another bedroom where there were three beds. But there was still room in the corner, and with the first overlocker arrived, and that was a major addition to the family and to the business because it changed manufacturing extraordinarily, because until the overlocker became available, the entire process of sewing up was done by hand. And people used to come and take out outdoor work. And all the-- the knitting was done on a fully fashioned basis because nobody ever dreamt of needing a square piece of cloth, and then cut away because the yarn was too valuable to be contemplated to be cut away and thrown away. Excuse me. But once the overlocker came about, which had sewing and cutting as one activity, because that was the nature of the overlocker, then the entire thing changed in terms of production. And all the changed in terms of the number of people involved and employed, because one overlocker did the work of many other workers. But-- so it had the pride of place in that little corner behind one of the-- of the beds in that little nook sat a woman overlocker. And that was a place revered and-- and respected, because nobody-- the children didn't dare come too close and interfere with such a valuable piece of-- of the-- the latest equipment. And the-- the domestic lived, of course-- slept in the kitchen. It was a sort of a wooden bank sort of thing which turned into a sleeping bed for night time, and then the daytime it used to have a cover on top. And that was working space for-- for the women to do their kitchen activities on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people came in during the day and worked in the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would think that in the-- in the work-- in the-- in the knitting room, there must have been six, seven, eight people there permanently. Mind you, also night shift. We have been-- the-- the of machines was limited. The investment in knit was a major burden. The-- and because most of it was hand driven, the-- these flat knitting machines were driven by hand in the early days. So the production was very slow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to utilize the time to make up for the slowness of the-- the production. So we had day shift and night shift. On Friday night when there was no activity in the factory, when-- when it was quiet, absolutely quiet, it was hard to sleep because we missed the music of the going machines. Who was employed?

speaker: interviewee
text: People. And all Jewish people were employed. One of them stands out in particular in my memory. He was a well built man with a beard, with the Hassidic cap, who was very outspoken about politics. And although he was a religious Jew, in terms of his political orientation he was a communist.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have got these type of personalities, and-- and-- and the people that-- that are almost a contradiction in terms, theoretically. Yet those were, because the reality of life was such that the-- the-- the manufacturer managed to make a living. The-- the worker in those days had so much harder of a--

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so there is no-- that-- not to be-- not to be amazed at such a juxtaposition of religious orientation and political, almost counter-ideology. But that was-- that was part of it. As I said, in the second room there was the overlocker. The-- the dining room was an open shop door. So people are coming and going all the time. Sometimes it was quiet. Sometimes there were more than one person. And if you would consider the fact that in the olden days, to find the appropriate garment was in itself a major decision. But you have to add in terms of sociological interaction the fact that after they have picked the garment and asked for the price, they started bargaining because it wasn't the case that you-- and in our-- our place, my father has introduced the rule that there was no bargaining. He would not-- he quoted a price and you would not budge. But that doesn't mean, or it didn't mean to the people who came to buy, who were used that wherever they went-- or they went to buy shoes, they went to buy whatever it is, they had to bargain because it was part of the process. They would have considered themselves foolish and-- and almost below-- below acceptance of their selves to be so-- so naive to accept the price without even trying to bargain.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that in itself was a-- an extraordinary sight to-- to watch and to listen and to see the-- the give and take of-- of-- of trying to-- to push the price down, and Father or whoever acted on his behalf, to hold on to his. And-- and it was quite-- quite a scene every time it occurred, and I never tired of it. Can you tell me about the suburb in which you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the word 'suburb' in terms of the city of Bialystok, or for that matter any city of that-- because Bialystok was a city of 100,000 people, which in European terms, pre-war term-- terms, was a very, very substantial city. Because when I-- when I went, let's say, in 1959 to Geneva, Geneva didn't have many more people than Bialystok had, and Geneva was a world renowned center of-- of political importance and-- and so on and so forth. It had nicer-- nicer environment. It had a beautiful lake, which Bialystok didn't have. But as far as number of people are concerned, 100,000 people was a very-- a very substantial-- substantial city. But-- but there-- there were outlying parts of the city, obviously. But as I've pointed out in the beginning, we lived right at the heart of the center of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was not suburb. Whatever the nature of the city was, it was to some degree agrarian in the sense of structures of-- of the types of-- yes, there were certain parts of streets where-- or certain streets which were more elaborate and more expensive and more exclusive. But by and large, it-- the differences in living standards, with few exceptions, were reasonably average.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was Kupiecka, and like other streets of that nature. Because on one end, Kupiecka touched on-- on-- on Pilsudkiego, which was one of the two major streets of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our neighbors--

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of nationality or religion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was Jewish. You had to work very hard, apart from the policeman, the postman, and on a-- and the-- and the-- and the man who looked after the-- the-- the-- the-- the house-- the-- the housing generally, as a sort of a caretaker, which in our case it did not apply. But where there are bigger-- bigger number of flats, there was a caretaker in-- everything was Jewish. Everything was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews lived in Bialystok at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of the 100,000 people, 70,000 were Jews. But when I say 100,000, it was-- or-- it was including the outer laying fringes of the city, which were included into-- or just tacked onto the city itself for political reasons by the-- by the Polish authorities. Because if you would have taken the city as-- on its own, the factor, it would have been 90% Jewish. So they added on the-- the fringes of the city, which were already peasantry and so on. So the-- the-- the mix and the-- and the ratio between the Jewish people and the non-Jewish people at least was somewhere alleviated to only 2/3 out of-- of almost 100%.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- of course, I remember Father's mother who lived with us, of course. That was Bobe. And I also remember my mother's father, who lived with his son because having had our grandmother with us, Father was-- said no room for more than that. And that was interesting because my father did not know his father. My grandfather-- that's my paternal grandfather-- died at age of 33 of consumption, which was not a rarity in those days. It was not a rarity.

speaker: interviewer
text: So much so that I have got my grandfather's name, which, as you know in Jewish-- in Jewish way, you don't name a child after a living person. So when I was born, my grandfather was-- not only that I didn't know him, my father didn't remember him. Because my father was three when-- when his father passed away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when my grandmother found herself, you can barely imagine, a-- a woman, whatever she was, at the age of 30 or 28, whatever it is, left with two little children and very little else. And she had to bring them up on her own in-- and it was quite-- quite an achievement on her part to bring up her two sons. The older son was a-- a genius class, brilliant mathematician. He was a-- a-- an accountant by profession. And he was so recognized that before the first World War when in Russia proper, or in Moscow in particular, the Jews could not come to live unless they had a special permit for-- for excellence in their profession. He had such a permit, and he lived in Moscow serving as a-- an accountant to a firm there. But most of the time that I remember, he was with us in Bialystok. He had his own family, of course. And he used to come every Saturday to visit his mother, Saturday afternoon, which was a set piece of-- of family interaction. And we as children looked forward to it. And even if the discussion usually turned down always on the same subject. And-- and they women were about talking about their-- their domestics and all those things, as it is normal, the things that are on people's minds. My grandfather, on the-- that's my mother's father-- was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he was-- he had cataracts on both his eyes. He was a scholar. And on-- although he could hardly read, he had to put his-- his hand over his eyes to shade it to be able to-- to fetch anything or catch anything in small print. He used to give a daily schul, a daily a lesson in Talmud by heart to go on up-- to his grown up congregation without fail. And when I was at the age of 11, 12, the summer months when all the boys were running around with their-- what sort of games they could find in the street, my father insisted that on the Sunday mornings I had to go every day to my grandfather's home. And he used to sit with me in the-- in the attic and teaching-- giving me a lesson in Talmud, day in and day out, for a couple of hours every day. Never looking into-- into the volume. Never looking into the book, and so on. But otherwise, he was in retirement, living with his son's family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Judel, or Yehudal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As I've indicated a minute ago, he was an orphan. And he grew up with his older brother. His older brother must have been two or three years older than himself. And their primary education was probably more interspersed with-- with Jewish studies than a straight out primary school education as such. Obviously. But at the age-- at age of 14, he had to go out to work because Grandmother managed to make a living, or whatever you may call it. She has opened a little corner stall of groceries. And that was the source of sustenance for her and for her two sons. And the story used to be told that when she had to go into the kitchen to prepare a meal and the older son used to come into the-- into the shop to wait for a customer, which may or may not have arrived during that generation, he used to spend the time exercising on the-- on the white wrapping paper in-- with abstract mathematical exercises. And so time she had to come to-- to roll something up, and it was covered in-- in-- in figures and so on and so forth. But Father worked as a-- in a textile shop where-- as a-- as a-- a-- a young helper, whatever it is, at age of 14, obviously. And then slowly, he learned the trade and-- and Bialystok, as we'll most probably talk about it later on, was an industrial city. And textile manufacturing was its hallmark.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody's wish and desire and ambition was to be a textile manufacturer. So having had such limited [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] chance in his early youth and life, that was his dream. And he ultimately-- ultimately became a textile manufacturer, both a set of spinning-- spinning machines which are huge, you know, the spinning-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a sort of, in the olden days-- even now, it takes most probably the length of the entire thing in those days. And-- but he didn't last long because the war years, first World War years immediate-- immediately after the war, things went so bad that he-- he just couldn't carry on. He had to give it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a religious man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was. Absolutely religious, in the most simple and most uncompromising way. That it was the norm in those days. And although he did not have a father to instruct him in it, but the family tradition was in itself very compelling because it so happens that we belong to one of the most illustrious families in [INAUDIBLE] for the last 500 years. Some of the most prominent Talmudic interpreters, people whose words and-- and work is included in the Talmud volumes, are our grandparents. And it went right down the line. It went right down the line from the Mashhad. And I'm mentioning it on record on purpose because those who know would know what-- what it stands for. And the [INAUDIBLE], the [INAUDIBLE]. And the entire line of the Brisk rabbis. The Brisk Linear, it was called. The Solovejcziks. You know, Rabbi Solovejczik of-- of Boston, the-- the greatest authority of our generation, just passed away a few years ago. That is the line of rabbis. Rabbi Chaim Solovejczik was the chief rabbi of Warsaw, and so on to Solovejczik. And when I was in 1940 in Lithuania, I was the guest of the chief rabbi of Lithuania, whose first name was the same as my uncle's name, my father's brother's name, Rov Avram Dov Shapiro. And the name of-- of Rabbi Shapiro, that-- known as the Kovno Rov, the Rabbi of Kovno, was renowned. And-- and Yehuda Bauer, in his-- in his historical work about the Holocaust makes reference to-- to his role during the-- the-- the-- the-- the liquidation of the Kovno ghetto, of course, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those were the-- the-- the antecedents and the background which made religious behavior, religious conviction, a byword in the pattern and understanding of life as an ongoing unquestioned relative. Thank you, Israel. We'll end tape one now. Thank you.
